claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I'd like to ask about Alex's current situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he in school, college, sixth form, or doing an approved training course?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in school or college anymore. He left education and isn't doing any training courses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I need to understand a bit more about Alex's current activities.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?** And if so, was this within the last 20 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces. He just left school and went straight into work.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask about his work situation to determine your eligibility.

**How many hours per week is Alex working in his paid job?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He works full-time - it's 24 hours a week or more actually. He's got a proper job now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me evaluate the situation based on the rules:

- Alex is 17 years old (under 20)
- He has left approved education or training
- He has not registered with a careers service or armed services
- He is working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment

According to the Child Benefit rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Eligibility Decision: Not Eligible**

I'm sorry, but you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Alex is 17 years old (which would normally allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit until he's 20), the rules state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week **and** is no longer in approved education or training.

Since Alex has left education, is not doing any training, and is working 24 hours or more per week, you would not qualify for Child Benefit for him.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he returns to approved education or training and reduces his working hours), you may be able to claim again at that point.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which would normally qualify for Child Benefit as he is under 20. However, Alex has left approved education or training and is now working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment. According to the Child Benefit rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex meets both of these conditions (not in approved education/training AND working 24+ hours per week), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. While Alex's age would normally qualify, he has left education and is working full-time (24 hours or more per week). Under Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child aged 16-19 starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old, which would normally qualify for Child Benefit as he is under 20. However, Alex has left approved education or training and is now working 24 hours or more per week in paid employment. According to the Child Benefit rules, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex meets both of these conditions (not in approved education/training AND working 24+ hours per week), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 17 years old. While Alex's age would normally qualify, he has left education and is working full-time (24 hours or more per week). Under Child Benefit rules, payments stop when a child aged 16-19 starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
